coredata.dc:title: Preparation of novel high performance recoverable and natural sunlight-driven nanocomposite photocatalyst of Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs
coredata.dc:creator: Aghamali, Ali
Khosravi, Morteza
Hamishehkar, Hamed
Modirshahla, Nasser
Behnajady, Mohammad A.
coredata.dc:description: This work describes a sunlight-active photocatalyst capable of magnetically separating prepared by depositing various amounts of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on the Fe3O4/C/TiO2 (FCT) nanocomposite. The photocatalytic activity of the resulting composites was investigated in the decolorization of methylene blue (MB), under UV, visible and sunlight irradiation. In the composites, the amorphous carbon interlayer is devised to prevent photodissolution of the Fe3O4 core. To coat the TiO2 nanoparticles on the insulated carbon surface of the magnetic core, a vapor phase hydrolysis method was used while a hydrothermal method was used to deposit N-CQDs on the Fe3O4/C/TiO2 nanocomposite surface. The prepared nanocomposites were characterized by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry, diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM). The results revealed that the N-CQDs content of the FCT composite has a great influence on the photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite. So that, the photocatalyst FCT/N-CQDs−4.5 wt% resulted in 95.5% MB decolorization within 80 min sunlight irradiation while pure TiO2 and FCT provided 20.3% and 31.8% decolorization, respectively. In addition, the results showed that sunlight has a significant effect on the photocatalytic activity of the composite, compared to visible and UV light irradiation. Moreover, the composite photocatalysts possessed superparamagnetic properties due to their good saturation magnetization value, i.e. 24.9 emu g−1. As a result, the developed composite photocatalyst was recovered by applying an external magnetic field without losing its activity even after five cycles. In general, this study provides new insights into the fabrication of a new generation of TiO2-based photocatalysts to overcome the main drawbacks of conventional TiO2 photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Sunlight driven
Recoverable nanocomposite photocatalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FE-SEM-EDX and TEM analysis of composite samples
DRS and PL analysis of samples
Stability and reusability of the nanocomposite photocatalyst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the financial support of Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences ( 95/115 ), the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council (INIC) [ 6958 ], Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University and Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FESEM image of N-CQDs and (b) XRD pattern of N-CQDs.
(a) DLS analysis of N-CQDs and (b) TEM image of N-CQDs.
(a) EDXS analysis of N-CQDs and chemical composition of N-CQDs is shown in the inset of the figure (a), and (b) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of N-CQDs in aqueous solutions. Insets show photographs of N-CQDs in aqueous solution under visible (left) and UV (right) light. (b) Excitation-dependent PL spectra of the N-CQDs excited at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Normalized PL emission of N-CQDs.
(a) Up-converted emission of N-CQDs excited by different excitation wavelengths and (b) Normalized excitation-dependent UCPL emission of N-CQDs.
XRD patterns of (a) Fe3O4/C, (b) Fe3O4/C/TiO2 and (c) Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs.
FESEM images of Fe3O4/C, Fe3O4/C/TiO2 and Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs; TEM images of Fe3O4/C, Fe3O4/C/TiO2 and Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs.
EDXS analysis of (a) Fe3O4/C, (b) Fe3O4/C/TiO2, (c) Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs and related chemical compositions is shown in the inset of the figure (a-c).
FTIR spectra of (a) Fe3O4/C, (b) Fe3O4/C/TiO2, and (c) Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs.
(a)UV–vis DRS spectra of the as-prepared TiO2, FCT/N-CQDs with different amount of N-CQDs.(b) The plot of Kubelka-Munk function versus the energy of light for the calculation of Eg.
PL spectra of N-CQDs and FCT/N-CQDs-4.5%, FCT/N-CQDs-0.0% composite photocatalyst.
Room temperature Magnetic hysteresis curves of Fe3O4/C and Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs nanocomposite (inset: separation of particles dispersed in water by a magnet in 30 s).
Comparison of decolorization rate of MB in three types of light irradiations in present of different amounts of N-CQDs.
Schematic illustration of the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the FCT/N-CQDs composites.
Recycling and reusing of Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs-4.5% photocatalysts for the photocatalytic decolorization of methylene blue dye solutions under natural sunlight.
XRD patterns of Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs-4.5% composite before (top) and after (bottom) fifth cycling and reusing photocatalytic experiments.
Estimated optical absorption band gaps of TiO2 and FCT/N-CQDs-x composites.
Comparison of photocatalytic activity and kinetics parameter of different amounts of N-doped CQDs composite, TiO2 and un-doped composite, for UV, solar, and visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Preparation of novel high performance recoverable and natural sunlight-driven nanocomposite photocatalyst of Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work describes a sunlight-active photocatalyst capable of magnetically separating prepared by depositing various amounts of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on the Fe3O4/C/TiO2 (FCT) nanocomposite. The photocatalytic activity of the resulting composites was investigated in the decolorization of methylene blue (MB), under UV, visible and sunlight irradiation. In the composites, the amorphous carbon interlayer is devised to prevent photodissolution of the Fe3O4 core. To coat the TiO2 nanoparticles on the insulated carbon surface of the magnetic core, a vapor phase hydrolysis method was used while a hydrothermal method was used to deposit N-CQDs on the Fe3O4/C/TiO2 nanocomposite surface. The prepared nanocomposites were characterized by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry, diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM). The results revealed that the N-CQDs content of the FCT composite has a great influence on the photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite. So that, the photocatalyst FCT/N-CQDs−4.5 wt% resulted in 95.5% MB decolorization within 80 min sunlight irradiation while pure TiO2 and FCT provided 20.3% and 31.8% decolorization, respectively. In addition, the results showed that sunlight has a significant effect on the photocatalytic activity of the composite, compared to visible and UV light irradiation. Moreover, the composite photocatalysts possessed superparamagnetic properties due to their good saturation magnetization value, i.e. 24.9 emu g−1. As a result, the developed composite photocatalyst was recovered by applying an external magnetic field without losing its activity even after five cycles. In general, this study provides new insights into the fabrication of a new generation of TiO2-based photocatalysts to overcome the main drawbacks of conventional TiO2 photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic composite materials have gained an increasing interest since they are a promising solution for the energy and environmental challenges. These materials have been studied by many scientists to develop efficient photocatalytic systems that can be used as reusable multifunctional composite photocatalysts with improved photocatalytic activity [1]. Among various photocatalysts, nano-scale semiconductor materials, such as TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs), have attracted a considerable deal of attention due to their potential environmental applications in purification of air and wastewater [2–4]. TiO2 has been extensively utilized in heterogeneous photocatalysis since it is non-toxic and low-cost and demonstrates a high photocatalytic activity under ultraviolet light [5,6]. Despite the presence of commercial TiO2 particles with acceptable photocatalytic capability, TiO2 has not been widely used in commercial or industrial water refinement systems. The reason can be related to the fact that the TiO2 particles that are commonly employed for photocatalytic degradation of wastewater are associated with several unavoidable disadvantages which restrict their applications. First, the wide band gap of TiO2 (3.0–3.2 eV) significantly limits its light harvesting ability. So that, it can be activated only by UV or near-UV radiations (λ ≤ 380 nm), which accounts for nearly 5% of the sunlight spectrum, while 45% of sunlight is composed of visible light [7,8]. In other words, TiO2-based photocatalytic processes cannot take advantage from using sunlight as an energy source, completely. Therefore, to enhance absorption of energy from the full spectrum of sunlight, the absorption boundary of TiO2 should be moved to the visible range. The second important disadvantage that limits the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 is the low quantum yield of its excitons due to its fast electron-hole recombination [6,9]. The third disadvantage refers to the issue that photocatalytic reactions are often carried out in suspensions of submicron semiconductor materials. While immobilization of photocatalysts is effectively used to retrieve and reuse the photocatalysts, the performance of anchored photocatalysts is generally less than that of suspended photocatalytic particles [10–12]. Consequently, anchoring the particles is not an appropriate solution for solving the problem of separating TiO2 particles from aqueous solutions and reusing them.

To overcome the mentioned problems and improve the photoactivity of TiO2, an option is using TiO2 composites. In the last few years, extensive researches have attempted to solve the major drawbacks of TiO2, as a photocatalytic material. For example, in order to overcome the deficiency of the relatively large band gap of TiO2, and shift its absorption edge from the UV region to visible light, some reports have suggested introducing nonmetals [6,13–17], metals [18–21], metallic oxides [6,22,23] and quantum dot, such as CdSe, [24–27] into the matrix of TiO2. Such modification can decrease the recombination of the electron-hole pairs and improve absorption of visible-light by TiO2. Nevertheless, most photocatalytic composite materials suffer from low utilization of sunlight, high recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons, toxicity and instability, which extremely limit the general performance and practical applications of TiO2 photocatalysis. Therefore, enhancing the performance of TiO2-based composite photocatalysts requires more robust approaches.

One potential approach is using carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are one of the newest nano-allotropes of carbon. CQDs were discovered in 2004 as a fractional product in the electrophoretic purification of the carbon nanotubes that were derived from arc discharge soot [28]. In recent years, many good aspects of CQDs have been unraveled including its outstanding ability of light harvesting and exclusive transfer of photogenerated electrons [29–31]. More importantly, CQDs exhibit both down and up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) behaviors. In UCPL, energy of the emitted photon is higher than the energy of the excited one. Consequently, CQDs can act as an energy convertor and utilize the full spectrum of solar light [32–34]. These features have motivated applying CQDs to photocatalysis processes.

Nonetheless, electron transfer in the interior parts of CQDs is poor and hinders their use in photocatalysis. Fortunately, their photocatalytic activity can be effectively promoted by adjusting their electronic levels through doping nitrogen and amine. Recently, doping CQDs with nitrogen atoms has been confirmed to be a feasible approach for improving the optical properties of CQDs and achieving excellent optical properties since the nitrogen-doping process can tune the intrinsic electronic and surface properties of CQDs [35–38]. Therefore, synthesis of novel CQDs with noticeable optical performance and combining of CQDs with an appropriate semiconductor, such as TiO2, not only extends the photo-harvesting range of the semiconductor but also enhances transfer of the photogenerated electrons. Recently, the unique features of CQDs have been addressed to report several semiconductor/CQDs composite materials, e.g. TiO2/CQDs, Fe2O3/CQDs and Cu2O/CQDs. The reported studies have declared that CQDs/semiconductor composites present photocatalytic efficiencies that are significantly higher than un-doped conventional photocatalysts [39–41].

On the other hand, as aforementioned, TiO2 particles cannot be easily recovered from reaction mediums. To solve the reusability issue, two possible methodologies have been established, i.e., immobilization of titanium dioxide onto different supports and synthesis of magnetic TiO2 photocatalyst [6,42,43]. Magnetically separable photocatalysts can be simply separated from a blended suspension by applying an external magnetic field, which facilitates their reuse. To enable magnetic photocatalyst recovery, the produced photocatalysts should contain a magnetic core, e.g., Fe3O4, NiFe2O4, γ-Fe2O3 or CoFe2O4. Among these magnetic particles, Fe3O4 has attracted much attention duo to its low toxicity, outstanding magnetic properties and high chemical stability [44,45]. However, the interaction of Fe3O4 with TiO2 influences the photocatalytic activity of TiO2, leads to considerable Fe3O4 photodissolution and increases the prevalence of electron-hole recombination [46]. To overcome these problems and improve the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2, a carbon or silica insulation layer can be considered to isolate the magnetic material from TiO2 [30,47]. Encapsulating magnetic cores with amorphous carbon not only improves separation of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs but also provides an amorphous layer of carbon with the –COOH and –OH functional groups, which can carry a tunable TiO2 shell and prevent photodissolution of the magnetic core [48,49].

While many researches have been performed to modify the surface of TiO2 and promote its photocatalytic performance, almost no effort has been made to expand the photocatalytic activity of magnetic TiO2 particles under natural sunlight via modifying its surface by N-CQDs [50–52]. Though some studies have reported multifunctional systems that respond to UV or visible light irradiations, few studies have concerned the influence of the full spectrum sunlight [53–55]. In recent years, there is a great concern about constructing multifunctional photocatalysts with improved visible light photoactivity and recyclability [32,56,57]. Therefore, development of the photocatalytic systems that are both magnetically recoverable and sunlight active is motivating. In this respect, magnetic visible or sunlight derived TiO2 photocatalysts have been explored to overcome the two important problems of photocatalyst retrieval and sunlight exploitation, which restrict the photocatalytic application of TiO2 in the fields of environmental contaminants and energy sources. Core-shell nanocomposite photocatalysts incorporate the properties of two or more chemical substances and rely on their synergistic effects, which may result in exclusive properties [58]. Therefore, core-shell photocatalysts with magnetic and visible or sunlight photoactive properties provide a powerful alternative solution for overcoming the limitations and drawbacks of conventional photocatalysts and achieving an ideal photocatalyst [59].

With respect to the outlined drawbacks and solutions, magnetic particles were chosen as the core material for separation purposes. Also, any potential improvement in the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible or sunlight was studied by employing different contents of N-doped CQDs. In this way, N-doped CQDs/TiO2 magnetic photocatalysts were prepared and evaluated to determine if they can present a very high photocatalytic activity in decolorization of aqueous methylene blue (MB) under sunlight irradiation. Furthermore, the performance of this composite photocatalyst under UV, visible and natural sunlight irradiations was compared with the performance of TiO2 and the Fe3O4/C/TiO2 (FCT) composite. It should be noted that this study was mainly devoted to enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the FCT composite by loading N-doped CQDs and investigating the role of N-CQDs in advancing the photocatalytic oxidation of MB under UV, visible and natural sunlight radiations. Evaluating the influence of different irradiations on the TiO2 composite aided to decide how light activates a composite photocatalyst and how a proper light source should be selected to decolorize MB, as the target pollutant. To the best our knowledge, no research has concentrated on devising a new generation of magnetic TiO2 particles doped with N-CQDs, to date.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA·H2O), ferrous chloride (FeCl2·4H2O), sodium acetate (NaAc), and starch were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and titanium (IV) n-butoxide (TBOT), absolute ethanol, and methylene blue (MB), were purchased from Merck. The water used in all experiments was purified via a Millipore system (18.3 M Ω cm−1). All the materials were used as received without further purification.

The N-CQDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal carbonization procedure. In a typical one-step synthesis procedure, 0.44 g citric acid was dissolved into 20 mL of ultrapure water, followed by addition of 0.15 g glycine under stirring. Then, the above colorless mixture was decanted into a stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon-lined of 30 mL volume and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The product was left to cool down naturally, then the solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water by a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 2 KDa) to remove the unreacted precursors that did not contribute in the reaction. Finally, a transparent light-yellow liquid was obtained.

The magnetic Fe3O4/C (FC) nanocomposites were synthesized using a one pot hydrothermal method in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Frist, 3.75 g starch was dissolved in 150 mL of H2O with vigorous mechanical stirring at 80 °C in a water bath until formed a homogeneous viscous solution. Subsequently, 3.354 g FeCl2·4H2O and 9.1315 g of NaAc were added in viscous solution of starch with vigorous agitating for 30 min at 80 °C in a water bath formed a homogeneous dark red solution. Then, the solution was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (200 mL capacity) for hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 20 h. Later cooling to ambient temperature, the black precipitate product was separated by a magnet and washed several times with water and ethanol. The as-prepared samples were dried overnight at 50 °C in oven.

0.20 g Fe3O4/C powders was added into 20 mL absolute ethanol solution and sonicated for ca. 20 min in an ultrasonic bath to obtain a homogenous colloidal solution than, 400 µL TBOT was added into above dispersion dropwise, than the mixture sonicated for 15 min that was then decanted immediately into the vapor phase hydrolysis apparatus (VPHA). 3 mL deionized water was situated at the bottom of VPHA as liquid phase to produce vapor at raised temperature, accordingly the liquid water did not interact with TBOT straight away and the intense hydrolysis was eluded. The closed VPHA was heated to 200 °C and maintained for 20 h. Then, the VPHA was cooled to room temperature. The as-prepared powder was collected by magnet and washed with absolute ethanol and deionized water for three times and was exsiccated in oven at 50 °C. Subsequently calcined at 450 °C for 2 h.

Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs was produced by a hydrothermal method. 0.1 g of obtained core shell composite was dispersed in a solution of distilled water (10 mL) and ethanol (5 mL). The mixture suspended under sonication for 30 min, then composites with various amount of N-CQDs (0, 1.5, 3.0, 4.5, 6.0 and 7.5 wt%) were mixed under sonication for another 30 min and then was poured into a Teflon- lined autoclave and held at 140 °C for 3 h. The resultant composites were recovered by an external magnate, rinsed with pure water several times and dried at 50 °C during 24 h. Finally, labeled as FCT/N-CQDs–x (x = 0.0, 1.5, 3.0, 4.5, 6.0 and 7.5 wt%).

In this study, we used a model pollutant (methylene blue) solution with a certain concentration under UV, visible light, and natural solar light irradiation in order to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs core-shell composites photocatalyst. The source of visible light was a 500 W halogen lamp with a cutoff filter to exclude radiation under 450 nm such that to make sure that just visible light was used for the irradiation of the dispersion. The UV light source was a 15 W UV lamp that was placed beside a quartz cylindrical reaction vessel. The sunlight intensity (54 ×103 ± 5000 lx) was measured by digital lux-meter during July to August 2016 (11 a.m. to 4 p.m.). In a typical trial, after dispersing 300 mg of FCT/N-CQDs-x composite photocatalyst in 150 mL of MB aqueous solution (20 mg L−1), the mixture was agitated in the dark for 1 h to ensure the establishment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium. All photocatalytic experiments were accomplished in a batch mode 350 mL cylindrical quartz photoreactor (3 cm of diameter and 50 cm of height). Then, the suspension was continuously bubbled by an O2 gas apparatus, which was used to provide oxygen and complete mixing of the reaction solution at ambient temperature. Afterward, the solution was exposed to three types of light irradiation, individually, followed by collecting the suspension (5 mL) at regular intervals to measure the MB removal percentage. The reduction in the absorbance rate at 664 nm wavelength, related to the general peak for the absorption spectra of MB, was applied to control the amount of decolorization of MB and the photocatalysis performance of the prepared samples with regard to irradiation time.

XRD, FT-IR, DLS, FESEM, TEM, and EDX were used to characterize the composition, structure, and size of the as-synthesized N-CQDs. According to FESEM images shown in Fig. 1a, the prepared N-doped carbon quantum dots are spherical and are 3–6 nm in diameter. The obtained N-CQDs are dispersed well in water, presenting a yellow solution that can be retained for several months without any detected accumulation. Fig. 1b shows the XRD pattern of N-CQDs. The observed extensive diffraction peak width is attributed to the small size of the N-CQDs. The XRD diffraction pattern of the N-CQDs presents a peak at 2θ = 19.3, which is indexed to the diffraction of graphite [002] crystalline planes, displaying a d spacing of 0.46 nm. This value is also somewhat larger than that of graphite, which has a d spacing of 0.335 nm along the (002) direction. Besides, the weak broad peak at ca. 2θ = 30–36 indicates the partial graphitization of the CQDs [60,61]. To further confirm the size of prepared N-CQDs, Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) study was also performed. Fig. 2a shows the size distribution histogram obtained from DLS, which is 3 nm on average. The size and morphologies of the as-prepared N-CQDs were characterized by TEM and shown in Fig. 2b, which exhibited a spherical geometry with an average size of about 4.5 nm. These observations further indicate that the N-CQDs agglomerate easily in solid form but stay isolated in water.

Also, to verify the chemical composition of the synthesized N-CQDs, elemental analysis was taken using the Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDXS) analysis and presented in Fig. 3a. FTIR spectra of N-CQDs in Fig. 3b reveals two significant peaks at 3438 cm−1 and 1660 cm−1, which correspond to O-H and C˭O starching vibrations, respectively. Moreover, the peaks at 3389 cm−1 and 1492 cm−1could be indexed to N–H vibrations. The peaks at 1395 cm−1, 1046 cm−1, and 2927 cm−1are attributed to the amide C–N, C–O, and C-H, respectively. The stretching vibration peaks of C˭C at 1563 cm−1 suggest the establishment of an unsaturated aromatic ring with the graphitic assembly in the hydrothermal treatment process. Furthermore, to investigate the optical properties of the N-CQDs, UV–visible absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of N-doped carbon quantum dots in water solution are presented in Fig. 4a, which presents the main absorption band located at ca. 350 nm. The band is related to the carbonyl and amine functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs. These groups, in turn, are attributed to the n-π * transition and a weak absorption peak situated at 254 nm, suggesting the π-π* transition of carbon core [62,63]. In addition, the obtained N-CQDs appears as pale transparent yellow under visible light and clear blue in 365 nm using the UV lamp (Fig. 4a). To further investigate the optical attributes of as-prepared N-CQDs, a detailed PL investigation was performed using different excitation wavelengths. Fig. 4b and c illustrate the normal and normalized PL spectra of N-CQDs. Also, the as-prepared N-CQDs show an excitation-dependent PL comportment. While the excitation wavelength was altered from 250 to 400 nm, the PL peaks were shifted from 414 to 484 nm. An extensive emission placed at 438 nm was observed in the emission spectrum with an excitation wavelength at 350 nm. It is worth noting that the emission spectra have Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of about 72 nm, which is attributed to the narrow size distribution of the N-CQDs. This result is in agreement with those presented in Figs. 1a and 2.

Apart from the strong PL feature, the N-CQDs show clear upconversion PL properties. As displayed in Fig. 5a-b, when the excitation wavelength changes from 600 to 800 nm, the up-converted emission peak shifts from 410 to 456 nm. Furthermore, it is established that the fluorescent emission peaks are usually broad and dependent on excitation wavelength and the peaks shift to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength is increased from 600 to 800 nm. Interestingly, the intensity of the peaks increases first and then decreases with increasing excitation wavelength. The intensity of the peaks at the 700–775 nm regions is higher than that in other regions. The brightness of the photoluminescence (PL) was estimated in terms of the emission quantum yield at 350 nm excitation. The detected quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be about 49.5%, with quinine sulfate as a reference, which is higher than those of numerous CQDs earlier reported [60,64].

XRD was carried out to characterize Fe3O4/C, Fe3O4/C/TiO2, and Fe3O4/C/TiO2/N-CQDs nanocomposites in order to determine the crystal structure and the composition of the as-prepared samples ( Fig. 6). The XRD patterns of FC, FCT, and FCT/N-CQDs-4.5% nanocomposites present the same set of characterization peaks (30.5°, 35.8°, 43.5°, 53.8°, 63.0°, and 62.6°) corresponding respectively to [220, 311, 400, 422, 511], and [440] lattice planes of the cubic phase of Fe3O4 (JCPDS card no. 01-075-0449). All the diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 were maintained after the TiO2 deposition, suggesting that the effects on the crystalline structure of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were negligible during the hydrothermal treatment. After interaction with titanium precursor and calcination at 450 °C, some peaks related to TiO2 emerged. The peaks (25.68°, 38.47°, 48.13°, and 54.55°) corresponding to the [101, 103, 200], and [105] lattice planes are consistent with the values on the JCPDS card (No: 01-0562), indicating the characteristics of anatase phase of TiO2 and signifying the formation of well-crystallized TiO2. The broad natural peak of Fe3O4 and deposited TiO2 are an indication of the small crystallite size. The mean size of TiO2 and Fe3O4 crystallites can also be defined from the broadening of corresponding XRD peaks using the Scherer's formula (Eq. (1))[65]:
(1)
D
= K
λ
/
β
cos
θ
,
where λ is the wavelength of the X-ray radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm), K is the Scherrer constant (K = 0.89), θ is the angle of the X-ray diffraction peak (for TiO2 and Fe3O4, θ = 12.85° and 17.94°, respectively), β is the Full Width at Half-Maximum (FWHM) of the (101) and (311) related to TiO2 and Fe3O4 planes, respectively. The estimated crystallite size of TiO2 and Fe3O4/C were about 8.95 and 28.8 nm, respectively. No sharp diffraction peaks corresponding to carbon were observed because the carbon is of poor crystallinity and its content in the FCT composite is low. The mild diffraction peaks of Fe3O4/C and FCT at ca. 20° are due to the existence of amorphous carbon. After deposition of TiO2, the intensity of XRD peaks of Fe3O4 was declined because there is somewhat carbon and anatase TiO2 coated on it. Also, N-CQDs phases were not found in the XRD patterns of FCT/N-CQDs. This result could be attributed to the amorphous nature of carbon quantum dots, the low concentration of N-CQDs, or the proper dispersion of N-CQDs in the composite medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FE-SEM-EDX and TEM analysis of composite samples
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the as-obtained products were determined by TEM and FE-SEM analysis. Fig. 7 illustrates FE-SEM and TEM images of FC, FCT, and FCT/N-CQDs samples. The as-prepared samples were established to be nearly spherical in shape to some extent of agglomeration. TEM and FE-SEM of the FC nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 7a-b. The FC nanoparticles, which are composed of many tiny nanoparticles, attach firmly to one another to form the spheres. The results showed that the diameter of the accumulated particles is around 40 nm. The morphologies of the FCT and FCT/N-CQDs composites are revealed by SEM and TEM Fig. 7c-f. A shell of anatase TiO2 formed by VPH process was coated on the surface of Fe3O4/C. The surface of particles is unshaped and jagged. The diameter is increased up to 80–100 nm. Based on these values, the TiO2 shell thickness can be predicted to be 55 nm, indicating the formation of the TiO2 coating. The particles size measured by TEM and FESEM is in a good agreement with the calculation made according to the XRD data. After the hydrothermal treatment process for 3 h, the N-CQDs were capable to be incorporated in the surface of the TiO2 shell of the composite. Nonetheless, due to the small size of N-CQDs, their dispersal could not be seen clearly on the surface of FCT composites, especially in FESEM image, but TEM images clearly prove the successful doping of N-CQDs on the surface of the final composite. To confirm the chemical composition of the synthesized powders, elemental analysis for investigating the chemical purity of samples and indicating whether the N-CQDs are evenly distributed on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles were taken by Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDXS) analysis. The EDXS result obtained from FE-SEM shows the presence of Fe, Ti, O, C, and N in the FCT/N-CQDs composite, confirming the N-CQDs coated on the surface of TiO2. The EDX spectra of FC, FCT, and FCT/N-CQDs composites and results of element analysis are shown in Fig. 8.

It can be seen that the weight percentage of carbon was 14.21, 10.83, and 12.86 wt% in FC, FCT, and FCT/NCQDs, respectively. Moreover, nitrogen from the EDX analysis revealed that N-CQDs have been successfully decorated on the surface of FCT composite. The presence of nitrogen (3.01 at%) in FCT/N-CQDs-4.5 wt% composite nanoparticles and its absence in both of FC and FCT were confirmed by the EDXS analysis (Fig. 8c). In addition, mapping was used to inspect the distribution of the elements in the FCT/N-CQDs-4.5 wt% sample (Fig. S1). It was observed that the N-CQDs were homogeneously attached on the surface of FCT composites. Also, there are no unexpected elements being detected, indicating the purity of the samples. Fig. S1(f) shows the SEM image and mapping of FCT/N-CQDs-4.5 wt% sample.

FT-IR spectra of FC, FCT, and FCT/N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 9. As shown in Fig. 9a, FT-IR spectra of FC shows the broad band in the range of 3200–3600 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups. The band at 1230 cm−1 corresponds to the C–OH stretching and O–H bending vibrations and the bands at ca.1595 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 can be attributed to C˭C and C˭O vibrations, respectively, suggesting the carbonization of starch. These results specify the presence of plentiful hydrophilic functional groups on the carbon shell. The hydrophilic groups not only are able to improve the aqueous dispersion ability of Fe3O4/C but also impressively enhance the attraction between the carbon coated magnetite core and TiO2 shell [49]. The band at 568 cm−1is related to the Fe–O bond vibration of Fe3O4. Fig. 9b presents the FT-IR spectrum pattern of FCT in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. FT-IR spectra shows a broad peak in ca. 450–800 cm−1, which is attributed to the vibration of M-O (M = metal) functional group. The band appeared around 586 cm−1can be attributed to the vibration of the Fe-O functional group. Also, the absorption peak at 669 cm−1 corresponds to the Ti-O absorption peak. Notably, in the observed peak of FCT around 450–850 cm−1 bandwidths is wider than that of FC. This result can be related to the overlapping of Ti-O and Fe-O peaks. The peaks appeared at 958 cm−1 for the FCT sample could be associated with the existence of stretching vibrations of Ti-O-C bonds. Moreover, the bands at 1635 cm−1 belong to H-O-H bending vibration, demonstrating that many -OH groups are present in the sample as a result of TiO2 coating and showing the creation of TiO2 in the outlier of FC. The peak intensity of C˭O vibration was partly declined with TiO2 deposition on the carbon shell of Fe3O4/C, which can be attributed to the establishment of Ti-O-C from carbonyl functional groups on the surface of Fe3O4/C. This observation suggests the formation of a strong interaction at the interface of TiO2 and carbon [66]. For the FCT/N-CQDs nanocomposite (Fig. 9c), a change in the chemical character of the photocatalysts surface is observed after the hydrothermal treatment step of the N-CQDs. Some changes of the chemical character of the surface of TiO2 are also observed on the FT-IR spectrum after the deposition of N-CQDs. The broad absorption peak at ca. 3460 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H groups on the surface of the composite. This finding shows that plenty of hydroxyl and amino groups exist on the surface of TiO2. The observed peaks in the range of 1050–1228 cm−1 are related to N–Ti–O stretching vibration. The data clearly showed that the N-CQDs were well decorated on the surface of the TiO2 shell [67]. Moreover, the absorption bands at 2847 cm−1and 2923 cm−1are attributed to the C–H stretching vibration modes. The broad absorption band below 1000 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations, which indicates the overlapping of the Ti–O–Ti, Fe–O, and Ti–O–C vibrations bands [68,69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: DRS and PL analysis of samples
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-response ability and absorption range of sunlight are among the crucial factors affecting the photocatalytic performance photocatalyst. In order to reveal the functions of N-CQDs, the light absorption property of pure TiO2 and the FCT/N-CQDs composites with different N-CQDs contents were measured via diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). As shown in Fig. 10a, pure TiO2 and un-doped FCT exhibit no absorption above 410 nm, which agrees well with the essential absorption function of TiO2 [70]. While all FCT/N-CQDs composites display clear absorption in visible region ranging besides the strong UV absorption, the absorption intensity gradually increases in a certain range with increasing the amount of N-CQDs in the composites [71].

Meanwhile, the band gap of pure TiO2 and FCT composites with different amounts of N-CQDs calculated by means of Kubelka-Munk method changes from 3.12 eV to 2.51 eV (Fig. 10b and Table 1). It could be observed that the absorption edge of FCT/N-CQDs has been obviously shifted toward the visible light region, which indicates that the FCT/N-CQDs composite photocatalyst can significantly enhance light harvesting capability for producing more electron–hole pair and thus lead to an enhanced photocatalytic activity.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra as a powerful analysis tool have been widely used to confirm the recombination rate of electron/hole and charge transfer processes in photocatalysts. As can be seen from these spectra, high-intensity PL represents fast recombination possibility of electron-hole pairs [72]. PL spectra for N-CQDs, FCT/N-CQDs-4.5%, and un-doped FCT were comparatively investigated. As shown in Fig. 11, N-CQDs show a wide and strong emission peak at 500 nm under visible light excitation of 420 nm. For FCT/N-CQDs-4.5% composite, photocatalysts presented greatly reduced PL intensities than N-CQDs, while the PL emission intensity of un-doped FCT was insignificant. The lower PL intensity in the FCT/N-CQDs-4.5% under visible light indicates efficient transfer photoexcited electrons and a slow charge recombination [73]. These results demonstrate that the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes in excited N-CQDs is suppressed by TiO2 in FCT/N-CQDs-4.5% composites. In the other words, photoinduced electrons in excited N-CQDs were injected into TiO2. These findings are consistent with the results reported by Zhang et al. [35]. They indicated that PL emission of NCQDs/TiO2 is greatly weaker than NCQDs, while TiO2 itself has negligible emission.

The magnetic properties of the as-prepared samples were evaluated with a VSM. Fig. 12 illustrates the magnetization curves of FC and FCT/N-CQDs nanocomposite. Both samples show a negligible coactivity and remanence, indicating the superparamagnetic character of these materials. The core FC has a saturation magnetization value higher than 70.4 emu g−1, which is larger than that of FCT/N-CQDs (24.9 emu g−1) due to the presence of non-magnetic materials. The great diminution in Ms could be significantly related to the nonmagnetic TiO2 shell existing on the surface of FC, which reduces the magnetic moment. Nevertheless, this magnetic property is adequate, and the FCT/N-CQDs core/shell composite could be separated from media within 30 s upon the use of a permanent magnet and re-dispersed again when the magnet is removed completely.

In order to investigate the efficiency of the as-synthesized composites in the photocatalytic decolorization of MB and to explore the effect of different amounts of N-CQDs on its performance, the activity of the composites was compared with the activities of pure TiO2 and bare FCT composite, under visible, UV and sunlight irradiations. Fig. S2(a) compares the photocatalytic activity of the FCT composites containing different amounts of N-CQDs under irradiation of three photo-sources (visible light, sunlight and UV light) at different time intervals. As Fig. S2(a) indicates, the rate of the reaction is much higher when the FCT/N-CQDs composites are used, except the case of UV irradiation. Meanwhile the bare FCT and pure TiO2 samples show negligible photocatalytic activity under visible and sunlight irradiations. The related kinetics plots obtained under the described conditions are shown in Fig. S2(b). Analysis of the results indicates that photocatalytic decolorization of MB in the presence of FCT, FCT/N-CQDs composites and TiO2 follows pseudo-first-order kinetics under the radiations of the three photo-sources. Fig. 13 displays the rate of MB decolorization using the composite photocatalysts with different amounts of N-CQDs. In this study, the optimum amount of N-CQD was found to be 4.5 wt%. It can be observed that applying the optimum amount of N-CQD has a great influence on the photocatalytic performance of the FCT photocatalyst. This finding reveals that adding an appropriate amount of N-CQDs can enhance the photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite. In other words, the amount of the N-CQD particles should be carefully adjusted. Otherwise, incorporating a high content of N-CQDs into the composite would not be satisfactory for obtaining a higher photoactivity. In this respect, after introducing a small amount of N-CQDs (i.e. 1.5 wt%), the activity of FCT/N-CQDs enhanced extraordinarily while the un-doped FCT and pure TiO2 samples showed poor photocatalytic activity under sunlight and visible light. As it can be observed in Fig. 13, the photocatalytic activity of FCT/N-CQDs-4.5 wt% is higher than that of pure TiO2, un-doped and the other doped FCT composites, under sunlight irradiation. So that, after 80 min, the highest photocatalytic efficiency for decolorization of MB under natural sunlight irradiation refers to the FCT/N-CQDs-4.5 wt% composite and equals to 95.5% while the efficiencies of the pure TiO2 and FCT photocatalysts are about 20.3% and 31.8%, respectively. Table 2 gives the photocatalytic activity of the composites with different amounts of N-CQDs, pure TiO2 and FCT, under UV, sunlight and visible light irradiations.

The observed enhancement in the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 can be explained as follows. First of all, N-CQDs, as a photosensitizer material, shifts the range of light absorption by TiO2 from the UV region to the visible region through the newly formed Ti–O–C and N–Ti–O bonds between the N-CQDs and TiO2. Formation of such bonds is evident from the FT-IR spectra. Therefore, electrical combination between the π (LUMO) orbitals of the N-CQDs and the conduction band of TiO2 helps to hybridize N-CQDs with TiO2 to expand the photo-response range of TiO2 not only into the visible light region but also to the full spectrum of sunlight, as confirmed by the DRS measurements. In fact, introducing carbon energy levels to the band gap of TiO2 can effectively lower the band gap energy of TiO2. So that, the band gap of the composite photocatalyst would be narrower than the band gap of the un-doped TiO2 composites, which can lead to a red shift in the light absorption spectra of TiO2, as shown in Fig. 10(b) and Table 1 [74]. Since N-CQDs can exhibit UCPL, the wavelength of the light they emit would be shorter than the absorbed light's wavelength. As shown in Fig. 5a, N-CQDs can considerably improve absorption of sunlight. Hence, the range of the light wavelengths that the composite can absorb is developed into the visible region and even into the near infrared region. Accordingly, the presence of UV, visible and long-wavelength lights in the solar spectrum can enhance transfer of electrons to the conduction band of the TiO2 photocatalyst under natural sunlight. As a result, the rate of photodecolorization by the TiO2/N-doped CQDs composite obtained under solar irradiation is higher than that of the visible and UV light irradiations. It should be mentioned that, when an optimal amount of N-CQDs is doped into the composite, each one of TiO2 and N-CQD particles would be excited simultaneously. Therefore, when the N-CQDs that are dispersed throughout the TiO2 surface are present, N-carbon quantum dots aid as both acceptors and donors to form a new electric field and result in spatial separation of the photogenerated electrons and hole. In other words, the presence of N-CQDs in the composite photocatalysts expedites transfer of the photogenerated electrons from the conduction band of TiO2 into the N-CQD particles and declines electron-hole recombination. Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons and holes can stimulate the photocatalytic reactions of the composites [29,75]. This ability of N-CQDs is inferred from the trend of MB decolorization obtained upon incorporating different amounts of N-CQDs in the composite. The results show that when above 4.5% N-CQDs is incorporated in FCT, the photocatalytic activity reduces. In this condition, electron-hole formation decreases since a great portion of the surface of TiO2 is buried under the N-CQD particles and TiO2 loses its photocatalytic role. Consequently, TiO2 photoexcitation does not occur and the electron acceptance capability of N-CQDs would lose its effectiveness. In such case, just the carbon layer can be photoexcited to send electrons into the conduction band of TiO2, which induces a preclusion of exciton generation from the underlay of carbon and causes a photocatalytic performance that is lower than that of pure TiO2 under UV irradiation [76]. However, when an optimal amount of N-CDQs is doped into the composite, photoexcitation causes electron transition in TiO2, too. Since N-CQDs have the capacity of electron acceptance, the excited electrons can move from the conduction band of TiO2 to N-CQDs and postpone electron-hole recombination in TiO2. Therefore, the photocatalytic activity of the composite promotes in decolorization of MB.

Based on the above results and the reported literature [37,77–79], a plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic activity of the FCT/N-CQDs-4.5% composite under sunlight is schematically shown in Fig. 14. According to this mechanism, N-CQDs (1) serve as an efficient electron mediator and promote separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, (2) act as an excellent photosensitizer to sensitize TiO2, in which the photoinduced electrons transfer from excited N-CQDs to TiO2, (3) play important roles in utilizing sunlight by the means of their UCPL behavior, and (4) reduce the band gap of the FCT composite as observed in the DRS results (Table 1). We think that these multiple effects might occur simultaneously in the decolorization process. As shown in Fig. 14, when the suspension is exposed to sunlight, the electrons in the valance bands of TiO2 and N-CQDs can be excited to their CBs and generate holes. Then, the photogenerated electrons from TiO2 transfer to N-CQDs, as an acceptor. Meanwhile, the N-CQD particles can act as an electron donor and inject their photogenerated electrons into the CB of TiO2. As an outcome of the dual property of N-CQDs, they facilitate efficient separation of electrons and holes and enhance the photocatalytic activity of the resultant composites. Also, anchoring N-CODs on TiO2 can broaden the photoresponse range of the FCT/N-CQDs composites to visible light, which can improve the visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity of TiO2. This can be related to the electronic overlapping between the π states of N-CQDs and the conduction band states of TiO2. In addition, the UCPL property of N-CQDs can help harnessing the full spectrum of sunlight for excitation of FCT, generation of h+/e− pairs and, therefore, improving the photocatalytic activity of FCT. As a result, the holes (h+) photoinduced in the surface of the FCT composite can further oxidize H2O or OH− to produce hydroxyl radical (˙OH) while the dissolved oxygen molecules (O2) can react with the photogenerated electrons to yield superoxide radical anions (˙O2 −). Both these radicals are extremely strong oxidants for decomposition of MB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusability of the nanocomposite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The stability and reusability of the composite photocatalyst are important factors in large-scale application. In this study, the as-prepared photocatalyst of FCT/NCQDs-4.5 wt% was subjected to repeat cycles of MB decolorization. As shown in Fig. 15, the photocatalytic efficiency of FCT/N-CQDs-4.5 wt% nanocomposite decreased slightly in the fifth cycles. However, the decolorization percent of MB in the last cycle was still 92.6%. The decrease in the photocatalytic activity of the prepared nanocomposite after each cycle of the procedure may result from the imperfect magnetic separation of nanocomposite photocatalyst and the loss of photocatalyst during the reaction. The structural stability of the FCT/N-CQDs-4.5%wt% nanocomposite was also inspected by XRD after five successive runs. As shown in Fig. 16, after five successive photodecolorization operations, no observable changes in the XRD was established for the reused nanocomposites. These results suggest that the crystalline structure of the nanocomposite hasn’t been damaged, indicating its stability and reusability.

A new recoverable and sunlight-driven photocatalyst FCT/N-CQDs-x was successfully prepared and characterized by various techniques. The results showed that FCT/N-CQDs-x was successfully synthesized and N-CQDs doped onto the TiO2 surface of the composite. The incorporation of proper amounts N-CQDs onto TiO2 surface not only could significantly increase the photoresponse of TiO2 to sunlight spectrum but also a proper amount of N-CQDs in the nanocomposites improve the transfer of photogenerated electrons. Also, we found that the upconversion characteristic of N-doped carbon quantum dots led to the increased photocatalytic efficiency in sunlight spectrum. The photocatalytic reusability and stability of FCT/N-CQDs remained almost unchanged after reusing over several cycles. In this study, a new insight was provided to design a new generation of efficient photocatalyst of TiO2 for overcoming the main drawbacks of the conventional TiO2 photocatalyst to the eliminate pollution from water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support of Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences (95/115), the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council (INIC) [6958], Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University and Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mssp.2018.07.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Photochem. Photobiol. C: Photochem. Rev.
Synthesis and magnetorheological effect of Fe3O4-TiO2 nanocomposite
